title: Nexus Dashboard
description: Task management dashboard focused on planning clarity, productivity, and team visibility.

caseStudy.projectOverview: Nexus Dashboard is a SaaS-style task management interface built to help users plan work, track deadlines, and keep team priorities visible in one place.
caseStudy.problemPurpose: Many task tools become noisy as projects grow. I wanted to build a cleaner dashboard that keeps critical information visible and reduces friction in day-to-day planning.
myFocus: Designing a maintainable front-end architecture for a growing product
Creating fast task interactions with clear loading and validation states
Keeping the interface readable and accessible across devices
whyThisStack: Next.js supports a scalable routing and rendering model for SaaS-style products
TypeScript helps keep shared data models safe as features expand
React Query improves server-state handling and caching for dashboard workflows
Zod makes form and API input validation explicit and predictable
keyFeatures: Loading and error states for smoother task workflows
challengesTradeoffs: Balancing flexibility with UI simplicity as feature scope grows
Choosing where to optimise early versus waiting for real usage patterns
Keeping components reusable without over-engineering abstractions
whatILearned: A stable data model early on reduces refactor cost later
Performance and clarity are closely connected: less visual noise improves perceived speed
Consistent UX patterns matter more than adding many isolated features
nextSteps: Expand task planning workflows and data visualisation
Improve coverage for critical state transitions and user flows
Refine product-level features as the project evolves

title: The No-Manual Life
description: Accessibility-focused blog translating UI and a11y decisions into practical front-end guidance.

caseStudy.projectOverview: The No-Manual Life is an accessibility-focused blog where I break down front-end decisions into practical, product-ready guidance.
caseStudy.problemPurpose: A lot of accessibility content is either too basic or too abstract. This project translates accessibility principles into implementation patterns teams can apply more easily in real products.
myFocus: Writing clear case-style articles based on real implementation decisions
Building a content structure that is easy to scan and navigate
Applying inclusive defaults in typography, semantics, and interaction states
whyThisStack: MDX supports scalable, component-driven technical writing
Next.js keeps routing and content delivery simple for a growing publication
Tailwind CSS helps create consistent and accessible UI patterns quickly
axe-core and Lighthouse support accessibility validation during development
keyFeatures: Semantic page structure and keyboard-friendly navigation
Reusable content structure for technical writing
Progressive enhancement and performance-aware front-end choices
challengesTradeoffs: Balancing writing depth with readability for different audiences
Keeping content production consistent while maintaining structural quality
Choosing how much design polish to add without slowing publishing
whatILearned: Technical storytelling gets stronger when examples are tied to product outcomes
Clear content information architecture makes expertise more visible
Accessibility discussions are more useful when connected to delivery constraints
nextSteps: Publish structured series on forms, navigation, and performance
Add lightweight search and filtering by topic
Expand the project as both a learning laboratory and a professional showcase

title: Brev.ly
description: URL shortener project focused on lightweight UX, clear validation, and scalable product foundations.

caseStudy.projectOverview: Brev.ly is a URL shortener project built around a lightweight interface and a simple full-stack structure with separate client and server applications.
caseStudy.problemPurpose: Link tools are often either too basic or overloaded with features. The goal here is to provide an efficient core experience first, with room to scale later.
myFocus: Defining a clean short-link creation and management flow
Exploring validation and feedback patterns for fast interactions
Structuring the project in a way that can support future growth
whyThisStack: React and TypeScript keep UI behaviour predictable and easy to evolve
Node.js fits a lightweight API flow for link generation and redirects
Zod helps enforce clear input contracts across client and server boundaries
keyFeatures: Client and server split for clearer project structure
challengesTradeoffs: Choosing the right MVP scope before adding advanced features
Structuring the project clearly while it is still evolving
whatILearned: MVP definitions should protect delivery speed and product clarity
Validation strategy has a strong effect on perceived reliability
Foundational architecture decisions should support iteration, not perfection
nextSteps: Build the first complete end-to-end flow for link creation and redirect
Add clearer project documentation and live demo support
Expand the product only after the core experience feels solid

title: Widget Upload
description: File upload interface focused on feedback, validation, and a smooth production-like user experience.

caseStudy.projectOverview: Upload Widget Client is a front-end file upload interface built to provide a clear, fast, and accessible experience when interacting with upload APIs.
caseStudy.problemPurpose: File upload flows often seem simple until validation, progress feedback, and error handling need to work together cleanly. This project focuses on turning that interaction into a more polished, production-like experience.
myFocus: Designing a clear upload flow with strong feedback and error states
Building a scalable component structure for real product-like interactions
Keeping the experience accessible, responsive, and easy to maintain
whyThisStack: React and TypeScript support a predictable and maintainable UI architecture
Zustand keeps state management lightweight for interactive upload flows
Radix UI helps reinforce accessible interaction patterns
Motion improves feedback and transition quality without overcomplicating the UI
keyFeatures: Validation and error handling for invalid formats
challengesTradeoffs: Balancing smooth UX feedback with implementation simplicity
Handling validation and upload states without creating noisy UI behaviour
Keeping the architecture reusable while the product is still evolving
whatILearned: Upload flows require careful state design to feel reliable
Good feedback patterns improve perceived quality as much as visual polish
Accessibility matters even more in interaction-heavy components
nextSteps: Finish the remaining polish and integration details
Strengthen end-to-end flow between client and upload service
Expand testing coverage for critical upload states and edge cases
